text: A serum 25(OH)D value below 75.8 nmol/L is a risk factor for stress fractures in athletes.
Stress fractures affect approximately 20% of all athletic competitors.

Well-documented risk factors for stress fractures include female sex, white ethnicity, older age, taller stature, lower aerobic fitness, prior physical inactivity, greater training volume, thinner bones, vitamin D deficiency, iron deficiency, menstrual disturbances, and inadequate calcium intake.
The lower extremity is most commonly affected by stress fractures, particularly the tibia, metatarsals, and pelvis.
Daily supplementation with 800 IU vitamin D and 2000 mg calcium has been associated with decreased prevalence of stress fractures in athletes.
specific_value: 800 IU vitamin D + 2000 mg calcium daily

text: Athletes should maintain serum 25(OH)D levels of at least 30 ng/mL, restored through lifestyle optimization and oral supplementation up to 2000 IU per day if deficient.
Athletes should have laboratory evaluation of vitamin D-dependent calcium homeostasis including 25-OH-D3, calcium, creatinine, and parathyroid hormone levels.
